name: AWS Fargate (Serverless Container Compute)
what_it_does: Run containers on AWS without managing servers or clusters — Fargate is a serverless compute engine for ECS and EKS that provisions, scales, and manages the underlying infrastructure automatically.
use_cases: Launch containerized batch jobs on-demand without provisioning EC2 instances, paying only for the vCPU and memory consumed during task execution
Run ephemeral AI agent tasks as isolated Fargate containers, each with scoped IAM roles and no shared host surface
Scale a web API backend by deploying ECS services on Fargate with Application Load Balancer and auto-scaling policies based on CPU or custom metrics
Execute scheduled data pipeline jobs using EventBridge + Fargate ECS tasks to process S3 data on a cron schedule without persistent infrastructure
Isolate multi-tenant workloads by running each tenant's job in a separate Fargate task with per-task IAM execution roles and VPC security groups
not_for: Workloads requiring GPU acceleration — Fargate does not support GPU instances; use EC2 launch type or SageMaker instead
Long-running stateful services that need persistent local disk beyond ephemeral storage — Fargate tasks have a 200GB ephemeral limit and no persistent block storage
Cost-sensitive high-throughput compute where EC2 Spot instances would be significantly cheaper for sustained workloads
best_when: You want fully managed container execution with zero cluster administration, strong IAM-based isolation, and seamless ECS/EKS integration on AWS.
avoid_when: Your workload requires GPUs, persistent local storage, or extreme cost optimization via Spot EC2 capacity at scale.

auth.notes: AWS SigV4 signing via IAM roles or access key credentials. Two IAM roles matter: the task execution role (allows Fargate to pull images and write logs) and the task role (grants the container application its own scoped AWS permissions). Fine-grained actions include ecs:RunTask, ecs:DescribeTasks, iam:PassRole.

pricing.paid_tiers: Linux/x86: $0.04048/vCPU/hour and $0.004445/GB/hour. Linux/ARM64 (Graviton): ~20% cheaper. Windows containers carry a higher per-hour rate. Minimum billing is 1 minute; billed per second thereafter.
pricing.notes: No upfront cost or idle charge — you only pay while tasks are running. Data transfer and load balancer hours billed separately. Spot Fargate tasks available at ~70% discount with interruption risk.

performance.rate_limits: Fargate inherits ECS API rate limits: RunTask ~1 TPS per account (burstable), DescribeTasks ~40 TPS. Task launch cold start typically 10-30 seconds for image pull + initialization. Limits adjustable via AWS Support.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: ECS/Fargate API errors surface typed exceptions (e.g., InvalidParameterException, ClusterNotFoundException) with descriptive messages. However, task launch failures appear in a 'failures' array in a 200 response body rather than as HTTP errors, which agents frequently miss.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: RunTask is not idempotent by default — calling it twice launches two tasks. Pass the clientToken parameter (UUID) to make RunTask idempotent within a 10-minute window. Task definition registration is effectively idempotent (creates new revision if content differs).
known_agent_gotchas: RunTask returns HTTP 200 even when the task fails to launch — always inspect the 'failures' array in the response, not just the HTTP status code
Fargate tasks take 10-60 seconds to reach RUNNING state after RunTask returns; agents must poll DescribeTasks with exponential backoff rather than assuming immediate availability

The task execution IAM role and the task IAM role are different — missing the task execution role causes silent image pull failures; missing the task role causes permission errors inside the container
Fargate ephemeral storage defaults to 20GB per task; if your container writes large files it will silently fail at 20GB unless you explicitly configure storageConfiguration up to 200GB

Container stdout/stderr is not accessible through the ECS API — logs go to CloudWatch Logs via the awslogs driver and must be queried separately using the logGroup and logStream from the task definition

security_score_components.security_notes: Per-task IAM roles provide strong isolation between workloads. Secrets Manager and SSM Parameter Store integrations inject secrets at container startup without embedding credentials in task definitions. VPC-native networking with security groups at the task level. ECR image scanning and immutable image tags recommended. No shared kernel with other tenants — each task runs on isolated infrastructure.
